skill.name: module-health
skill.description: Assesses modular architecture health for Logseq Template Graph projects by reviewing module size, cohesion, documentation, and dependency signals.
skill.summary: Evaluate module balance, cohesion, and refactoring needs in Logseq Template Graph repositories.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command and weak-cryptography patterns, but review found no malicious intent, network exfiltration, credential access, or prompt injection. The main confirmed risk is that the skill includes Bash and PowerShell helper scripts that read local project files and run local commands to calculate module health. Publish with a clear warning that users should review scripts before running them in untrusted repositories.

medium_findings.title: Local Health Scripts Execute Commands and Read Project Files
medium_findings.description: The Bash and PowerShell helpers enumerate local module directories, count matching EDN entries, calculate scores, and print recommendations. This behavior is relevant to the skill, but users should review the scripts before running them because they execute local commands and read repository files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Executable Bash and PowerShell scripts are present and contain local command and filesystem operations. The reviewed context shows architecture analysis only, with no evidence of network access, credential access, or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Documentation Includes File-Changing Command Examples
low_findings.description: The examples include commands for creating module directories, running builds, validating output, and committing changes. These commands are presented as user-facing examples rather than hidden execution, so the risk is limited to users copying commands without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clear markdown content and are not executed automatically. They can still modify project files if copied and run by a user.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-risk weak cryptography detections point to prose about module descriptions, README quality, and checklist text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, cryptographic APIs, hashing code, or password handling in the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation text and checklist items, not cryptographic operations. Review found no code path that performs weak hashing or encryption.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Static Match Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The cited documentation section describes module dependency concepts and does not instruct the assistant to scan networks. No evidence found of curl, wget, HTTP requests, sockets, port scanning, or external endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no network APIs or shell network utilities. The line is part of an architecture metric description, so the static match is not a real network reconnaissance issue.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Uses Local Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The Bash helper uses shell features and commands such as grep, basename, arithmetic expansion, and bc while analyzing repository files. The operations are local and quoted, but they require the user to trust the script before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell script contains real command execution and filesystem traversal. The reviewed commands are scoped to local project analysis and do not show malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: PowerShell Script Enumerates Local Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The PowerShell helper uses Get-ChildItem, Test-Path, Select-String, and Join-Path to inspect local module files. This is expected for a health-check tool, but it is still an executable local analyzer.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cmdlets and file reads are directly visible and are used for module metrics. No evidence found of network calls, persistence, hidden execution, or credential collection.

content.value_statement: Large modular repositories can hide bloated, under-documented, or poorly balanced modules. This skill reviews module structure and produces focused refactoring guidance.
actual_capabilities: Counts classes and properties in Logseq Template Graph modules.
Scores modules using size, documentation, completeness, and ratio criteria.
Identifies bloated, small, empty, or poorly documented modules.
Suggests module splits, merges, and documentation improvements.
Provides Bash and PowerShell helper scripts for local health checks.
Produces summary tables and prioritized recommendations.
limitations: Designed for the Logseq Template Graph source layout.
Health scores are heuristics and require project context.
Dependency analysis may need manual review of EDN references.
Helper scripts read local files and should be reviewed before use.

use_cases.title: Audit Module Balance
use_cases.description: Review a modular repository before a release and identify modules that are too large, too small, or missing documentation.

use_cases.title: Plan Refactoring Work
use_cases.description: Turn architecture issues into prioritized split, merge, and documentation tasks with rough effort estimates.

use_cases.title: Review Template Contributions
use_cases.description: Check whether new classes and properties fit the intended module boundaries before submitting changes.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Quick Health Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner scan of the current repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Check module health for this repository. Summarize the overall score, module counts, and the top three issues.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect One Module
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review of a suspected problem module
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the misc module in detail. Group its classes by domain and suggest which groups should move into new modules.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Refactoring Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a module reorganization plan. Include proposed modules, files to update, validation steps, risks, and estimated effort.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Health Over Time
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare module health across recent commits. Show growth trends, score changes, and modules that are becoming harder to maintain.

output_examples.output: Overall health is 73 out of 100. The misc module is bloated, two modules are very small, and most core modules are stable.
The misc module contains several coherent domains. Communication, medical, financial, and education groups are strong candidates for separate modules.
Start by splitting the largest mixed module, then document small modules. Validate the build after each move and track the health score.

best_practices: Review generated recommendations against product goals before moving classes.
Run validation and build checks after each module reorganization step.
Document module purpose and ownership when creating or merging modules.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the health score as a replacement for architectural judgment.
Do not copy shell commands from examples without reviewing their effect.
Do not split modules only to improve scores if the new boundaries are unclear.

faq.question: What repositories does this skill support?
faq.answer: It is designed for Logseq Template Graph repositories that use source modules with classes, properties, and README files.

faq.question: Does it change files automatically?
faq.answer: The skill provides analysis and recommendations. The included examples may show commands, but users control whether to run them.

faq.question: Can it detect circular dependencies?
faq.answer: It can guide dependency review and report suspected coupling, but complex relationships may need manual confirmation.

faq.question: Why are helper scripts included?
faq.answer: The scripts provide a repeatable local health check for module counts, scores, and common issues.

faq.question: Is the risk level safe?
faq.answer: The reviewed skill is medium risk because it includes executable local scripts. No malicious behavior was found.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
